title: Disturbances of Respiratory Function
authors: George R. Washko, William M. Oldham

items.title: Efficiency of Gas Exchange in Alveoli
items.content: The efficiency of gas exchange in the lungs depends on the matching of ventilation and perfusion in alveoli. The lung's structure, with a large surface area (typically 70 m²) within a modest thoracic cavity volume (typically 7 L), facilitates this process through multigenerational branching of pulmonary arteries and bronchial airways.
keyPoints: Lung alveolar surface area is typically 70 m².
Thoracic cavity volume is typically 7 L.
Efficient gas exchange requires matching ventilation to perfusion in each alveolus.
Variations in tube lengths, calibers, gravity effects, tidal pressure fluctuations, and chest wall constraints can cause variations in ventilation and perfusion even in healthy individuals.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.title: Physiological Requirements for Effective Respiration
items.content: Effective respiration necessitates tidal lung ventilation to refresh alveolar gas, proportional perfusion relative to ventilation, and adequate diffusion of respiratory gases between alveolar gas and capillary blood. The system must also accommodate increased demands for oxygen uptake or CO₂ elimination due to metabolic needs or acid-base imbalances.
keyPoints: Tidal ventilation is essential for refreshing alveolar gas.
Perfusion must be proportional to the ventilation of each alveolus.
Adequate diffusion between alveolar gas and capillary blood is crucial.
The respiratory system must handle increased demands for oxygen uptake or CO₂ elimination due to metabolic needs or acid-base derangement.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.title: Understanding Transpulmonary Pressure in Lung Mechanics
items.content: Transpulmonary pressure is crucial for maintaining alveolar inflation and preventing lung collapse. It represents the difference between alveolar pressure ($P_{alveolus}$) and pleural surface pressure ($P_{pleura}$). This pressure must be equal or exceed to keep the lungs inflated, countering the natural tendency of the lung to collapse due to surface tension and elastic recoil.
keyPoints: Transpulmonary pressure is defined as $P_{alveolus} - P_{pleura}$.
The lung's natural tendency is to collapse because of surface tension at the air-liquid interface and elastic recoil.
At zero inflation pressure, normal lungs still retain some alveolar air due to radial pull keeping small airways open.

items.title: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) in Respiratory Mechanics
items.content: FRC is the equilibrium point of the respiratory system where the outward recoil of the chest wall balances the inward recoil of the lung. This balance results from differing mechanical properties: the lung becomes stiff at high volumes, while the chest wall stiffens at low volumes due to anatomical constraints.
keyPoints: FRC is the passive resting point where outward chest wall recoil equals inward lung recoil.
The elastic behavior of the chest wall differs markedly from that of the lungs; it stiffens at low volumes.
At FRC, negative intrapleural pressure offsets positive intrapulmonary pressure, yielding an airway pressure of 0 mmHg.

items.title: Role of Respiratory Muscles in Ventilation
items.content: Respiratory muscles are essential for overcoming the passive equilibrium at FRC, facilitating gas flow by altering pressures. Inspiratory muscles decrease pleural pressure to allow lung inflation, while expiratory muscles increase alveolar pressure to promote deflation.
keyPoints: Inspiratory muscles expand thoracic volume, decreasing pleural pressure and allowing airway opening pressure to exceed alveolar pressure for inhalation.
Expiratory muscles raise alveolar gas pressure above airway opening pressure, facilitating exhalation.
The normal passive respiratory system would remain at FRC without the action of respiratory muscles.

items.title: Understanding Dynamic Airflow Limitation in Pulmonary Diseases
items.content: Dynamic airflow limitation occurs due to the collapsible nature of bronchial airways during rapid exhalation, influenced by the Bernoulli effect. This phenomenon is crucial for understanding how diseases like emphysema and asthma affect maximal expiratory flow.
keyPoints: Dynamic airflow properties are significantly affected in pulmonary diseases.
Bernoulli effect: Increased velocity of air during rapid exhalation reduces intraluminal gas pressure, leading to airway collapse.
Maximal expiratory flow is influenced by lung volume, recoil pressure, and airway resistance.
In emphysema, reduced lung recoil pressure decreases maximal expiratory flows.
Asthma or chronic bronchitis narrow the airway lumen, reducing maximal expiratory flow.

items.title: Maximal Expiratory Flow and Its Clinical Implications
items.content: Maximal expiratory flow is a critical parameter in assessing lung function, influenced by factors like gas density, airway cross-section, and elastic recoil pressure. It varies with lung volume and is altered in conditions such as pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema.
keyPoints: Spirometry measures maximal expiratory flow from TLC to RV.
Maximal expiratory flow decreases with lung volume due to reduced lung recoil pressure.
Pulmonary fibrosis increases lung recoil pressure, elevating maximal expiratory flow relative to lung volume.
Emphysema reduces lung recoil pressure, lowering maximal expiratory flows.

items.title: Inspiratory Airflow Limitation and Its Causes
items.content: While inspiratory airflow limitation is rare due to diffuse pulmonary airway disease, it can occur with extrathoracic airway narrowing. The Bernoulli effect during inspiration promotes airway expansion, preventing significant limitations.
keyPoints: Inspiratory airflow limitation is uncommon in diffuse pulmonary diseases.
Negative pleural pressures during inspiration increase transmural pressure, aiding airway expansion.
Extrathoracic conditions like tracheal adenoma or posttracheostomy stricture can cause inspiratory airflow limitation.

items.title: Increased Work of Breathing in Disease
items.content: The work of breathing can significantly increase due to metabolic demands or mechanical loads. Diseases that reduce lung compliance, such as interstitial processes or fibrosis, and those increasing airflow resistance, like asthma or emphysema, elevate the effort required for ventilation.
keyPoints: Work of breathing increases with decreased compliance (e.g., fibrosis) or increased airway resistance (e.g., asthma).
Dynamic hyperinflation in severe obstruction elevates functional residual capacity, reducing lung compliance and increasing elastic work.

items.title: Ventilation Response to Exercise and Acidosis
items.content: During exercise or metabolic acidosis, ventilation increases primarily to eliminate carbon dioxide. This response is more efficient with increased tidal volume at lower exercise levels, while both tidal volume and respiratory rate increase at higher intensities.
keyPoints: Ventilation can increase >20-fold during exercise as a compensatory mechanism for CO2 elimination.
Normal ventilatory response to low-level exercise involves increasing tidal volume without changing the respiratory rate.

items.title: Anatomic and Functional Dead Space in Ventilation
items.content: The anatomic dead space is the airway volume that does not participate in gas exchange. Functional dead space can increase if parts of the lung receive no blood flow, affecting CO2 elimination efficiency.
keyPoints: Alveolar ventilation ($V_A$) is calculated as $[V_T - V_D] 	imes RR$.
CO2 elimination depends on alveolar ventilation and the difference in CO2 fraction between inspired air (0%) and alveolar gas (~5.6% or 40 mmHg).

items.title: Assessing Ventilation Adequacy via Paco2
items.content: The adequacy of alveolar ventilation is reflected in the arterial $P_{CO_2}$ ($Paco_2$). Normal $Paco_2$ is approximately 40 mmHg. Alveolar hyperventilation is indicated by a $Paco_2$ significantly below this threshold, while alveolar hypoventilation is suggested when $Paco_2$ exceeds it.
keyPoints: $Paco_2$ reflects alveolar ventilation adequacy
Normal $Paco_2$: ~40 mmHg
Alveolar hyperventilation: $Paco_2$ much below 40 mmHg
Alveolar hypoventilation: $Paco_2$ exceeds 40 mmHg

items.title: Understanding Alveolar Gas Equation for PaO2 Estimation
items.content: The alveolar gas equation allows estimation of alveolar oxygen tension, incorporating factors like inspired oxygen fraction ($FiO_2$), barometric pressure ($P_{bar}$), water vapor pressure at body temperature ($PH_2O = 47 	ext{ mmHg} 	ext{ at } 37^	ext{oC}$), and respiratory quotient (R). This equation highlights that severe arterial hypoxemia is unlikely due to alveolar hypoventilation at sea level with normal lungs, but risk increases with altitude.
keyPoints: Alveolar gas equation: $PaO_2 = FiO_2 	imes (P_{bar} - PH_2O) - Paco_2/R$
Influences on $PaO_2$: $FiO_2$, $P_{bar}$, $PH_2O = 47 	ext{ mmHg}$ at $37^	ext{oC}$, and R
Normal respiratory quotient (R) for typical American diet: ~0.85
Severe arterial hypoxemia unlikely from alveolar hypoventilation at sea level with normal lungs

items.title: Oxygen Uptake is Perfusion Limited in Normal Lungs
items.content: In healthy lungs, the uptake of oxygen is primarily limited by blood flow through alveolar capillaries rather than the rate at which oxygen diffuses across the alveolar membrane. This efficient diffusion allows hemoglobin to become fully saturated with oxygen after traveling just one-third the length of an alveolar capillary.
keyPoints: Oxygen uptake is 'perfusion limited' in normal lungs.
Hemoglobin becomes fully saturated by the time it travels one-third the length of an alveolar capillary.
Diffusion limitation occurs rarely, such as at high altitudes or during maximal exertion.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.title: Ventilation/Perfusion Mismatch and its Impact on Gas Exchange
items.content: Efficient gas exchange requires matching ventilation (V) to perfusion (Q). In disease, V/Q mismatch can lead to areas of the lung being either ventilated but not perfused or perfused but not ventilated. This mismatch causes arterial hypoxemia that is often refractory to supplemental oxygen in shunt conditions.
keyPoints: V/Q heterogeneity exists even in normal lungs due to gravity and anatomical differences.
Extreme V/Q mismatches include ventilation of unperfused lung (physiologic dead space) and perfusion of nonventilated lung (shunt).
Shunted blood lowers arterial $P_{ao}$, making hypoxemia refractory to supplemental oxygen.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.title: Arterial Hypoxemia from V/Q Heterogeneity is Responsive to Oxygen Therapy
items.content: In conditions with widened V/Q ratios due to lung disease, blood perfusing relatively underventilated alveoli becomes partially oxygenated. This results in arterial hypoxemia that can be improved with supplemental oxygen, unlike shunt-induced hypoxemia.
keyPoints: Widened V/Q ratio distribution is common in lung diseases.
Perfusion of relatively underventilated regions causes incomplete blood oxygenation.
Arterial hypoxemia from V/Q heterogeneity responds to supplemental oxygen.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.title: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis: Increased Elastic Recoil
items.content: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis increases lung recoil, reducing TLC, FRC, RV, and FVC. Maximal expiratory flows are relatively increased due to greater lung recoil and radially outward traction on bronchi.
keyPoints: Increased lung recoil lowers TLC, FRC, RV, and FVC.
Maximal expiratory flows are elevated relative to lung volumes.
Airway resistance remains normal despite increased flow.
Diffusing capacity is markedly reduced due to destruction of pulmonary capillaries.
Oxygenation may be severely reduced by persistent perfusion of underventilated alveolar units.

items.title: Moderate Obesity: Chest Wall Abnormality
items.content: In moderate obesity, the chest wall's outward recoil is blunted by adipose tissue, leading to a fall in FRC. TLC remains unchanged unless massively obese, and RV is normal but may be reduced in massive obesity.
keyPoints: Outward recoil of the chest wall is blunted by weight of adipose tissue.
FRC falls due to preserved inward lung recoil overbalancing reduced outward chest wall recoil.
TLC typically unchanged; RV normal unless massively obese.
Mild hypoxemia may occur due to airway closure in dependent lung portions.
$ DL_{CO}$ remains normal unless obstructive sleep apnea induces pulmonary arterial hypertension.

items.title: Acute Asthma: Airflow Obstruction
items.content: In acute asthma, airflow obstruction is caused by smooth muscle constriction and inflammation in bronchi. Flow-volume loop shows 'scooping' due to reduced airflow at lower lung volumes.
keyPoints: Luminal narrowing raises frictional resistance and reduces airflow.
Flow-volume loop shows 'scooping' due to reduced airflow, especially at lower lung volumes.
TLC usually remains normal; FRC may be dynamically elevated.
RV often increased due to exaggerated airway closure at low lung volumes.
$ R_{aw}$ is usually elevated; mild arterial hypoxemia present but responsive to oxygen supplementation.
$ DL_{CO}$ is normal or mildly elevated.

items.title: Mechanisms of Airflow Obstruction in Severe Emphysema
items.content: In severe emphysema, airflow obstruction is primarily due to decreased elastic recoil of the lungs. This results in pulmonary hyperinflation characterized by elevated Total Lung Capacity (TLC). Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) and Residual Volume (RV) are significantly increased because of loss of lung elasticity and dynamic hyperinflation, respectively.
keyPoints: Loss of lung elastic recoil leads to pulmonary hyperinflation.
Elevated TLC is a hallmark of severe emphysema.
FRC is severely elevated due to loss of lung elastic recoil and dynamic hyperinflation (auto-PEEP).
RV is very severely elevated because of airway closure and prolonged exhalation time.
Both Forced Vital Capacity (FVC) and Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 second (FEV1) are markedly decreased.

items.title: Impact of Emphysema on Gas Exchange
items.content: In emphysema, the loss of alveolar surface and capillaries reduces Diffusing Capacity for Carbon Monoxide (DLco). While arterial hypoxemia is not typically seen at rest unless emphysema is very severe, during exercise, PaO2 may drop sharply if pulmonary vasculature destruction prevents adequate increases in cardiac output.
keyPoints: Loss of alveolar surface and capillaries reduces DLco.
Arterial hypoxemia usually not seen at rest until emphysema is very severe.
During exercise, PaO2 may fall if pulmonary vasculature destruction prevents sufficient increase in cardiac output.
Venous admixture through low V/Q units significantly lowers mixed arterial oxygen tension during exercise.

items.title: Understanding Lung Volume Measurement Techniques
items.content: Spirometry is essential for assessing relative lung volume changes but cannot determine absolute volumes. Two methods are used to measure absolute lung volumes: inert gas dilution and body plethysmography. Inert gas dilution, which uses gases like helium or neon, can underestimate true lung volumes in areas with poor ventilation due to airflow obstruction. Body plethysmography measures the compressibility of thoracic gas volume using Boyle's law.
keyPoints: Spirometry reveals relative but not absolute lung volumes.
Inert gas dilution may underestimate lung volumes in obstructed regions.
Body plethysmography uses Boyle's law to measure FRC and calculate TLC and RV.
Lung volume determinants include height, age, and sex.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Evaluating Airflow with Spirometry
items.content: Spirometry is crucial for measuring airflow dynamics. During an FVC maneuver, the patient exhales from TLC to RV rapidly and forcefully, ensuring flow limitation. The FEV1/FVC ratio is a key metric; however, it can be misleading in cases where increased RV reduces FVC, making the ratio appear normal despite airflow obstruction.
keyPoints: FEV1 measures air exhaled in the first second during an FVC maneuver.
The FEV1/FVC ratio is reduced in airflow obstruction but may appear normal if FVC is decreased by increased RV.
Spirometry includes spirogram and flow-volume loop plots to visualize volume, flow, and time relationships.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Interpreting Flow-Volume Loops in Airflow Obstruction
items.content: Flow-volume loops help identify the site of airflow obstruction. In lower airway obstructions like asthma and emphysema, the loop shows a scooping pattern due to rapid flow decrease with declining lung volumes. Fixed upper-airway obstructions result in inspiratory or expiratory flow plateaus.
keyPoints: Flow-volume loops display volume vs. flow relationships during spirometry.
Lower airway obstruction causes a characteristic scooping of the loop.
Fixed upper-airway obstruction leads to inspiratory and/or expiratory flow plateaus.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Respiratory Muscle Strength
items.content: Respiratory muscle strength is evaluated by measuring pressures during maximal effort breathing against a closed shutter. Pressures greater than ±60 cmH₂O at Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) indicate adequate respiratory muscle function, reducing the likelihood of weakness contributing to ventilatory dysfunction.
keyPoints: Pressures >±60 cmH₂O at FRC are considered adequate for respiratory muscle strength.
Spirometry in the supine position can better identify inspiratory muscle weakness due to increased diaphragmatic work.
FVC decreases by 3–8% from upright to supine in normal subjects, and by 10-25% in those with diaphragmatic weakness or neuromuscular disease.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.title: Evaluating Gas Exchange Using DLco
items.content: The diffusing capacity of the lungs for carbon monoxide (DLco) measures gas exchange across the alveolar membrane. It is influenced by surface area, hemoglobin levels, and alveolar membrane thickness. DLco decreases in conditions like pulmonary fibrosis or emphysema but may increase in asthma or polycythemia.
keyPoints: DLco uses a small amount of CO to assess gas exchange during a 10-s breath hold.
Decreases in DLco are seen with thickened or destroyed alveolar membranes, reduced pulmonary vasculature, or low hemoglobin levels.
Conditions like asthma and polycythemia can cause elevated DLco.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: Where does the diffusion and equilibration of respiratory gases between blood and gas occur in the lungs?
items.explanation: Diffusion and equilibration of respiratory gases occur across an extremely thin membrane in the lung alveoli, separating blood flowing through alveolar wall capillaries from alveolar gas.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.question: What is required for efficient gas exchange in the lungs?
options: Unidirectional airflow and bidirectional blood flow
Matching ventilation to perfusion in each alveolus
Equal distribution of gravity effects on all alveoli
items.explanation: For efficient gas exchange, the fresh gas ventilation of a given alveolar must be matched to its perfusion.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.question: What is the typical surface area provided by the alveoli for blood-gas diffusion?
items.explanation: The alveoli provide an enormous surface area, typically around 70 m², for blood-gas diffusion within the thoracic cavity.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.question: Which component of the respiratory system is responsible for generating force?
items.explanation: The neuromuscular system generates the force required for breathing.

items.question: What is the natural tendency of the lung due to surface tension and elastic recoil?
items.explanation: The lung naturally tends to collapse because of both surface tension at the air-liquid interface and elastic recoil.

items.question: At what point is the functional residual capacity (FRC) attained?
options: When the lung pressure exceeds chest wall pressure
When the outward recoil of the chest wall balances the inward recoil of the lung
When the neuromuscular system ceases to function
When alveolar gas pressure is zero
items.explanation: FRC is attained when the outward recoil of the chest wall is balanced by the inward recoil of the lung.

items.question: What happens to small peripheral airways as the lung deflates during exhalation?
options: They remain open due to radial pull
They are pulled open less and eventually close, trapping some gas
items.explanation: As the lung deflates during exhalation, small peripheral airways are progressively pulled open less and eventually close, trapping some gas.

items.question: What primarily causes maximal expiratory flow to fall with lung volume under normal conditions?
options: Decreased elastic recoil pressure of the lung
Dependence of lung recoil pressure on lung volume
Reduced frictional resistance in pulmonary airways
items.explanation: Maximal expiratory flow falls with lung volume primarily due to the dependence of lung recoil pressure on lung volume.

items.question: Which phenomenon reduces airflow during rapid exhalation below expected levels if only frictional resistance were considered?
options: Dynamic airflow limitation due to collapsible bronchial airways
items.explanation: Dynamic airflow limitation occurs because the bronchial airways are collapsible, reducing airflow below expected levels.

items.question: In which condition is maximal expiratory flow elevated in relation to lung volume?
items.explanation: In pulmonary fibrosis, increased lung recoil pressure at any lung volume elevates maximal expiratory flow in relation to lung volume.

items.question: What is the effect of the Bernoulli principle on inspiratory airflow limitation?
options: It causes significant inspiratory airflow limitation due to increased airway collapse.
It prevents inspiratory airflow limitation by promoting airway expansion.
It has no impact on inspiratory airflow limitation.
It increases frictional resistance during inspiration.
items.explanation: The Bernoulli effect promotes airway expansion during inspiration, preventing significant inspiratory airflow limitation due to diffuse pulmonary disease.

items.question: Which factor primarily determines the rate of ventilation in healthy individuals?
options: The need to eliminate carbon dioxide
Volitional needs such as playing a musical instrument
The compliance of the respiratory system
items.explanation: Ventilation is primarily set by the need to eliminate carbon dioxide.

items.question: What happens to the work of breathing when there is a reduction in respiratory system compliance?
options: It decreases due to reduced elastic load
It remains unchanged as compliance does not affect work
It increases because more effort is needed to ventilate the lungs
It fluctuates based on tidal volume
items.explanation: Reduced compliance increases the work required to overcome the elasticity of the respiratory system.

items.question: During which condition does dynamic hyperinflation occur, leading to a sensation of difficulty in inhaling?
items.explanation: Dynamic hyperinflation occurs with severe airflow obstruction, causing difficulty in inhaling.

items.question: What is the primary consequence of tidal volumes smaller than the anatomic dead space during quiet breathing?
options: No fresh gas enters the alveoli
items.explanation: When tidal volumes are smaller than the anatomic dead space, no fresh gas enters the alveoli.

items.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between alveolar ventilation and arterial PCO2 in a steady state?
options: Alveolar ventilation is independent of arterial PCO2.
Arterial PCO2 reflects the adequacy of alveolar ventilation.
Alveolar ventilation decreases as arterial PCO2 increases.
Alveolar ventilation and arterial PCO2 are inversely proportional but unrelated.
items.explanation: The adequacy of alveolar ventilation is reflected in arterial PCO2, which the respiratory controller maintains around ~40 mmHg.

items.question: What does a Paco2 value significantly below 40 mmHg indicate?
items.explanation: A Paco2 value significantly below 40 mmHg indicates the presence of alveolar hyperventilation.

items.question: What is the typical respiratory quotient (R) for a standard American diet?
items.explanation: The respiratory quotient (R = ∀CO₂/VO₂) is typically around 0.85 for a standard American diet.

items.question: Which factor primarily limits oxygen uptake from the lungs under normal conditions?
options: The rapidity with which oxygen can diffuse across the alveolar membrane.
The amount of blood transiting the alveolar capillaries.
The solubility of oxygen in plasma.
The inspired partial pressure of oxygen.
items.explanation: Oxygen uptake from the lung is said to be 'perfusion limited' rather than diffusion limited, meaning it's primarily limited by the amount of blood transiting the alveolar capillaries.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.question: Under which circumstances can oxygen uptake become diffusion-limited?
options: During normal breathing at sea level.
In interstitial lung disease with thickened alveolar walls.
When hemoglobin is fully saturated in the capillaries.
At low altitude with minimal exertion.
items.explanation: Oxygen uptake can become diffusion-limited in rare circumstances such as at high altitude or in interstitial lung disease where alveolar walls are thickened.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.question: What is the effect of ventilation/perfusion mismatch on arterial oxygen tension (PaO2)?
options: It increases PaO2 due to better gas exchange.
It has no effect on PaO2.
It decreases PaO2 by mixing partially reoxygenated blood with well-oxygenated blood.
It causes a linear increase in arterial oxygen content.
items.explanation: Perfusion of relatively underventilated alveoli results in incomplete oxygenation, lowering arterial PaO2 when mixed with well-oxygenated blood.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.question: Why is the arterial hypoxemia caused by shunt regions refractory to supplemental inspired oxygen?
options: Because raising FiO2 increases alveolar gas tensions in nonventilated alveoli.
Because hemoglobin saturation cannot be increased further in ventilated units.
Because it raises PaCO2, worsening hypoxemia.
Because the solubility of oxygen in plasma is high.
items.explanation: In shunt regions, raising FiO2 has no effect on alveolar gas tensions in nonventilated alveoli, making arterial hypoxemia refractory to supplemental inspired oxygen.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.question: In idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, what happens to the maximal expiratory flows relative to lung volumes?
options: They are reduced from normal values.
They remain unchanged from normal values.
They are elevated when considered in relation to lung volumes.
items.explanation: Maximal expiratory flows are elevated relative to lung volumes due to increased lung recoil and relatively increased airway diameters.

items.question: What is the effect of moderate obesity on FRC?
items.explanation: In moderate obesity, preserved inward lung recoil overbalances the reduced outward chest wall recoil, causing FRC to fall.

items.question: Which parameter remains normal in myasthenia gravis despite ventilatory restriction?
items.explanation: In myasthenia gravis, airway size and lung vasculature are unaffected, so both $ R_{aw} $ and $ DL_{CO} $ remain normal.

items.question: What is a common finding in the flow-volume loop during an episode of acute asthma?
options: There is scooping due to reduced airflow, especially at lower lung volumes.
The loop shifts toward higher absolute lung volumes.
items.explanation: Scooping of the flow-volume loop occurs during acute asthma due to reduction in airflow, particularly at lower lung volumes.

items.question: In severe emphysema, which parameter is primarily responsible for the marked elevation of residual volume (RV)?
options: Airway closure and prolonged exhalation time
items.explanation: RV is very severely elevated due to airway closure and because exhalation toward RV may take so long that RV cannot be reached before the patient must inhale again.

items.question: What phenomenon contributes to dynamic hyperinflation in severe emphysema?
items.explanation: Dynamic hyperinflation is related to auto-PEEP, which occurs when a new breath is initiated before the lung volume returns to FRC.

items.question: Which of the following best describes why arterial hypoxemia at rest is not typically seen in severe emphysema until it becomes very advanced?
options: Poorly ventilated acini are also poorly perfused
Normal alveolar surface area and capillaries
items.explanation: Arterial hypoxemia is usually not seen at rest because poorly ventilated emphysematous acini are also poorly perfused due to loss of their capillaries.

items.question: Which method of measuring lung volumes can underestimate true lung volumes due to poor ventilation in certain regions?
items.explanation: Inert gas dilution can underestimate true lung volumes because poorly ventilated regions may not contribute to the dilution of the inert gas.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What principle is used in body plethysmography to calculate thoracic gas volume?
items.explanation: Body plethysmography uses Boyle's law, which relates pressure and volume changes within the thoracic cavity.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: What does the FEV1/FVC ratio typically indicate in cases of airflow obstruction?
items.explanation: The FEV1/FVC ratio is typically reduced in conditions with airflow obstruction.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: In which condition does the flow-volume loop show a characteristic scooping due to lower airway obstruction?
items.explanation: In asthma, the flow-volume loop shows a characteristic scooping due to lower airway obstruction.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following pressures at Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) indicate adequate respiratory muscle strength?
items.explanation: Pressures >±60 cmH₂O at FRC are considered adequate for respiratory muscle strength.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: What is the effect of performing spirometry in the supine position on patients with diaphragmatic weakness?
items.explanation: Patients with diaphragmatic weakness suffer decrements from 10 to >25% when performing spirometry in the supine position.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.question: In which condition is the single-breath diffusing capacity (DLco) likely to be elevated?
items.explanation: Single-breath diffusing capacity may be elevated in asthma.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Blood flow through the lung is bidirectional.
items.explanation: Blood flow through the lung is unidirectional via a continuous vascular path.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.statement: The alveolar space must be ventilated tidally to freshen alveolar gas.
items.explanation: Tidal ventilation is necessary for the respiratory system to succeed in oxygenating blood and eliminating CO₂.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.statement: The elastic recoil of the lung decreases with inflation.
items.explanation: The elastic recoil of the lung increases with inflation, making it stiffer at high volumes.

items.statement: Functional residual capacity is achieved when the pressure in the alveoli exceeds the pressure at the airway opening.
items.explanation: FRC is achieved when the outward recoil of the chest wall balances the inward recoil of the lung, not based on alveolar versus airway pressures.

items.statement: Dynamic airflow properties of the lung are quantitatively important only at normal breathing frequencies.
items.explanation: Dynamic airflow properties become quantitatively important at markedly supraphysiologic breathing frequencies, not normal ones.

items.statement: In emphysema, reduced lung recoil pressure is a principal mechanism by which maximal expiratory flows fall.
items.explanation: Reduced lung recoil pressure in emphysema leads to decreased maximal expiratory flows.

items.statement: The work of breathing is minimal at rest due to low elastic and dynamic loads.
items.explanation: At rest, the respiratory muscles require minimal effort due to small elastic and dynamic loads.

items.statement: Dynamic pulmonary hyperinflation is primarily caused by inspiratory airflow obstruction.
items.explanation: Dynamic pulmonary hyperinflation is caused by expiratory, not inspiratory, airflow obstruction.

items.statement: Severe arterial hypoxemia rarely occurs as a pure consequence of alveolar hypoventilation at sea level while breathing air.
items.explanation: The alveolar gas equation implies that severe arterial hypoxemia is unlikely to occur solely due to alveolar hypoventilation at sea level.

items.statement: Capnography can only be used for invasive assessment of CO2 clearance.
items.explanation: CO2 clearance can be estimated noninvasively using capnography.

items.statement: Diffusion limitation of oxygen uptake is common under normal physiological conditions.
items.explanation: Oxygen uptake from the lung is usually 'perfusion limited' and not diffusion-limited under normal circumstances.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.statement: Supplemental oxygen can improve arterial hypoxemia caused by ventilation/perfusion mismatch.
items.explanation: Arterial hypoxemia induced by V/Q heterogeneity is typically responsive to oxygen therapy.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.statement: In idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, oxygenation is often severely reduced.
items.explanation: Oxygenation is often severely reduced due to persistent perfusion of relatively underventilated alveolar units.

items.statement: In myasthenia gravis, TLC is typically elevated.
items.explanation: TLC is low in myasthenia gravis because respiratory muscle strength is insufficient to fully expand the lungs.

items.statement: In severe emphysema, the flow-volume loop demonstrates marked scooping due to airway obstruction.
items.explanation: The marked scooping of the flow-volume loop in severe emphysema is due to loss of lung elastic recoil and not primarily from airway obstruction.

items.statement: During exercise, PaO2 may fall precipitously in severe emphysema if extensive destruction of the pulmonary vasculature occurs.
items.explanation: PaO2 can fall sharply during exercise if there is significant destruction of the pulmonary vasculature preventing adequate increases in cardiac output.

items.statement: Spirometry can reveal absolute lung volumes.
items.explanation: Spirometry discloses relative volume changes but cannot reveal the absolute volumes.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: In body plethysmography, the patient is instructed to pant against a closed shutter.
items.explanation: The patient pants against a closed shutter to measure pressure fluctuations for calculating thoracic gas volume.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.statement: DLco decreases with an increase in alveolar membrane thickness.
items.explanation: DLco varies inversely with alveolar membrane thickness.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.statement: Pulse oximetry can measure arterial blood gases directly.
items.explanation: Pulse oximetry determines hemoglobin saturation noninvasively, not arterial blood gases.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)

items.assertion: The matching distributions of ventilation and perfusion allow normal gas exchange.
items.reason: Variations in tube lengths, calibers, gravity effects, tidal pressure fluctuations, and anatomic constraints cause variations in alveolar ventilations and perfusions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason explains why matching distributions are necessary for normal gas exchange, not how they allow it.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · Disturbances of Respiratory Function

items.assertion: The normal passive respiratory system would remain at FRC without muscle action.
items.reason: Gas flows from high to low pressure, and the lung inflates when airway opening pressure exceeds alveolar pressure.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why the respiratory system would remain at FRC without muscle action.

items.assertion: The chest wall becomes stiff at high lung volumes.
items.reason: This is due to squeezing together of ribs and intercostal muscles, diaphragm stretch, and displacement of abdominal contents.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the chest wall stiffens at low volumes. The reason correctly describes factors contributing to stiffness but applies to low lung volumes.

items.assertion: Spirometry can assess dynamic airflow limitation.
items.reason: Spirometry involves inhaling fully to TLC and then forcibly exhaling to RV, measuring maximal expiratory flow at any lung volume.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why spirometry can assess dynamic airflow limitation.

items.assertion: Inspiratory airflow limitation is common in diffuse pulmonary airway disease.
items.reason: The Bernoulli effect during inspiration lowers pressure outside the airways, increasing transmural pressure and promoting airway expansion.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because inspiratory airflow limitation seldom occurs due to diffuse pulmonary airway disease; however, the reason is true.

items.assertion: The work of breathing increases during exercise.
items.reason: Ventilation increases to eliminate carbon dioxide produced by increased metabolic activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.

items.assertion: Functional residual capacity is reduced in severe airflow obstruction.
items.reason: Expiratory flows are insufficient to allow complete exhalation during tidal breathing.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; functional residual capacity increases, not decreases.

items.assertion: Oxygen uptake from the lung is limited by diffusion across the alveolar membrane.
items.reason: The aggregate alveolar membrane has a large surface area and minimal thickness, facilitating rapid diffusion.
items.explanation: While the alveolar membrane facilitates efficient diffusion, oxygen uptake is usually 'perfusion limited', not diffusion-limited.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.assertion: Arterial hypoxemia in shunt regions can be corrected by increasing inspired FiO2.
items.reason: Increasing FiO2 raises alveolar gas tensions in nonventilated alveoli, improving oxygenation.
items.explanation: In shunt regions, raising FiO2 has no effect on alveolar gas tensions in nonventilated alveoli, so hypoxemia is not corrected.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · GAS EXCHANGE

items.assertion: In acute asthma, TLC usually remains normal.
items.reason: TLC can sometimes be elevated in long-standing asthma due to air trapping.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.

items.assertion: In moderate obesity, RV is typically reduced.
items.reason: RV may be reduced in massive obesity due to increased chest wall adipose tissue and intraabdominal fat.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; RV is normal in moderate obesity but may be reduced only in massive obesity.

items.assertion: Inert gas dilution often underestimates true lung volumes.
items.reason: Regions of the lung that ventilate poorly may not receive much inspired inert gas and do not contribute to its dilution.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why inert gas dilution can underestimate true lung volumes.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: The FEV1/FVC ratio is always reduced in airflow obstruction.
items.reason: Airflow obstruction can also reduce FVC by raising RV, sometimes making the FEV1/FVC ratio appear normal.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason explains why the FEV1/FVC ratio might not always be reduced in airflow obstruction.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 1)

items.assertion: The single-breath diffusing capacity (DLco) is a measure of gas exchange across the alveolar membrane.
items.reason: This test uses carbon monoxide to determine the quantity crossing the alveolar membrane and combining with hemoglobin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Disturbances of Respiratory Function · FUNCTIONAL MEASUREMENTS (part 2)
